topic: What Developers Actually Use: JetBrains AI Tool Survey 2026

competitors.title: What 24,000 Developers Actually Use for AI Coding (JetBrains Data)
key_points: JetBrains surveyed 24,534 developers (April-June 2025), considered one of largest developer surveys
62% of developers now use at least one AI coding assistant
85% of developers use AI tools regularly in coding/development work
Top benefits: increased productivity (74%), faster repetitive tasks (73%), less time searching (72%)
Tool adoption: ChatGPT 41% (down from 49% in 2024), GitHub Copilot 30%, JetBrains AI Assistant ~15%, Cursor ~12%, Windsurf ~8%, Tabnine ~5%
Most developers use both ChatGPT (for exploration/debugging) and dedicated coding assistant (for in-IDE tasks)
Code quality is top concern (23%), with limited understanding of complex code logic (18%), privacy/security (13%)
46% of code in Copilot-enabled projects is AI-suggested, PR time reduced 9.6 days to 2.4 days (75% reduction)

competitors.title: Which AI Coding Tools Do Developers Actually Use at Work?
key_points: January 2026: 90% of developers regularly use at least one AI tool at work
74% of developers worldwide adopted specialized AI tools (coding assistants, editors, agents)
GitHub Copilot: 76% awareness, 29% adoption at work (40% in companies >5,000 employees)
Claude Code: 57% awareness (up from 31% in Apr-Jun 2025), 18% adoption at work (6x growth), 24% adoption in US/Canada
Cursor: 69% awareness, 18% adoption at work (shares 2nd place with Claude Code)
OpenAI Codex: 27% awareness, only 3% adoption at work
Google Antigravity: 6% adoption by January 2026 (launched Nov 2025)
Claude Code has highest loyalty: 91% CSAT satisfaction, 54 NPS score
ChatGPT chatbot: 28% use for coding tasks, Gemini 8%, Claude chatbot 7%
JetBrains ecosystem: AI Assistant (9%), Junie (5%), Central as unified agent control plane

stats.fact: 62% of developers use at least one AI coding assistant (JetBrains 2025 survey)
85% of developers use AI tools regularly in coding/development work (up significantly from previous years)
46% of code in Copilot-enabled projects is AI-suggested
Pull request time dropped from 9.6 days to 2.4 days (75% reduction) for Copilot users
January 2026: 90% of developers regularly use at least one AI tool at work
74% of developers worldwide adopted specialized AI tools (coding assistants, editors, agents)
Claude Code has highest loyalty metrics: 91% CSAT satisfaction, 54 NPS score
Only 3% of developers use OpenAI Codex for work despite 27% awareness
GPT-4 doesn't include updated experimental data. Missing data for GPT-4 included: experimental data.
Google Antigravity reached 6% adoption by January 2026 just 2 months after launch

angles: Tool adoption vs awareness gap: Many tools known but not widely used for work
Specialization trend: Developers moving from general ChatGPT to purpose-built coding assistants
Enterprise vs individual adoption patterns: Copilot dominates enterprises (>5K employees: 40% adoption)
Rapid growth of newcomers: Claude Code (6x growth), Google Antigravity (6% adoption in 2 months)
Developer concerns: Code quality (23%), limited understanding of complex logic (18%), skill degradation (11%)
Dual-tool usage patterns: Most developers use both ChatGPT and dedicated coding assistant for different tasks
outline: Introduction: Why developer AI tool surveys matter in 2026
Methodology: How JetBrains collects reliable developer data
Overall adoption: From 62% to 85% to 90% - the rapid progression
Tool-by-tool breakdown: Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Antigravity
Regional differences: North America vs global adoption rates
Enterprise patterns: Why Copilot still dominates large companies
Rapid risers: Claude Code's 6x growth story
Developer concerns: Code quality, skill degradation, privacy
Dual-tool usage: ChatGPT + dedicated assistant patterns
Impact on productivity: PR time reduction, code generation rates
Market implications: Consolidation vs specialization trends
Future outlook: Agentic workflows and open ecosystems
Actionable insights: What this means for tool builders
Conclusion: Balancing adoption growth with developer concerns
